title: Remembering a Star-Gazing Hero
story: Crowds gathered to honor Carl Sagan, the beloved astronomer who made space exciting for everyone. In 1997, on February 3, people came together at Cornell University to celebrate his life. story: A groundbreaking moment unfolded as Pope Francis stepped onto the sands of Abu Dhabi. In 2019, he became the first pope ever to visit the Arabian peninsula. title: Students Stand Up for Equal Education
story: Classrooms sat empty as students took to the streets. On February 3, 1964, Black and Puerto Rican students in New York City boycotted public schools. They were protesting unfair treatment and demanding better education. Nearly half a million students joined the peaceful protest, making their voices heard. This brave action helped spark important changes in the school system.

title: Blizzard Blankets the Big Apple
story: Snowflakes danced through the air, piling up faster than New Yorkers could shovel. In 1961, a massive blizzard hit New York City on February 3, dumping a whopping 17.4 inches of snow. This winter wonderland became the 6th largest snowfall in NYC history, transforming the bustling streets into a silent, white playground.
activity_question: How much snow fell during this 1961 blizzard in New York City?
bonus_fact: In metric units, the snowfall measured 44.2 centimeters.

title: Frozen Fun: Early Ice Hockey in England
story: Skates sliced across Mare Fen as teams from Swavesey and Over faced off in an early ice hockey match in 1857. title: Wisconsin Stands Up for Freedom
story: Boom! Wisconsin's Supreme Court made a bold move in 1855. On February 3, they declared the US Fugitive Slave Law unconstitutional. This law forced people to help catch escaped slaves. But Wisconsin said, 'No way!' They believed everyone deserved freedom. It was a brave step towards ending slavery in America.

title: Sun-Watching Satellite Takes Flight
story: Zoom! A special spacecraft blasted off on February 3, 1965. The Orbiting Solar Observatory 2 rocketed into space to study our nearest star - the Sun! This high-flying satellite circled Earth, peering at the Sun's mysteries from far above our planet's atmosphere. Scientists were excited to learn more about solar flares and other fiery secrets of our glowing neighbor.
